explanation_md: Every `Counter` constructor increments the single shared `s_count`. Three objects are created (`a`, `b`, `c`), so `s_count` ends up as 3. Because `s_count` is static, there is only one copy shared by all instances â€” each constructor call increments that same variable.

There is only one `s_val` regardless of how many `Box` objects exist. Writing `x.s_val = 42` modifies that single shared variable. Reading `y.s_val` reads the same variable, so it prints `42`. This surprises many learners who expect each object to have its own copy.

prompt_md: A class has a non-`inline` static member variable declared inside the class body as `static int s_count;`. Which statement is true?

options.a: The declaration inside the class is both the declaration and the definition â€” no other line is needed.
options.b: An out-of-class definition such as `int MyClass::s_count{ 0 };` is required to allocate storage.
options.c: The variable is destroyed when the last object of the class is destroyed.
options.d: Each object gets its own copy of `s_count`.

explanation_md: For a non-`inline` static member, the declaration inside the class only introduces the name; actual storage is allocated by the out-of-class definition (`int MyClass::s_count{ 0 };`). Without it, the linker reports an undefined reference. Option (a) is wrong because in-class declaration is not a definition for non-`inline` statics. Options (c) and (d) are wrong â€” static members have static storage duration (they live for the entire program) and there is only one shared copy.

prompt_md: Which of the following is the correct way to access a `public` static member variable `s_total` of class `Inventory` when you have an `Inventory` object named `inv`?

options.a: Only `Inventory::s_total` is correct; `inv.s_total` is a compile error.
options.b: Only `inv.s_total` is correct; `Inventory::s_total` is a compile error.
options.c: Both `Inventory::s_total` and `inv.s_total` are valid and refer to the same variable.

explanation_md: C++ allows accessing a static member either through the class name (`Inventory::s_total`) or through an object (`inv.s_total`). Both compile and both refer to the exact same single variable â€” using an object name is just syntactic sugar. `Inventory::s_total` is preferred in practice because it makes the static nature explicit and avoids confusion.

options.a: `static int s_count;` inside the class is a syntax error; static members cannot be declared inside a class body.
options.b: `Widget::s_count` is not a valid way to access a static member â€” you must use an object.
options.c: There is no out-of-class definition for `s_count`, so the linker cannot find its storage.
options.d: The constructor increments `s_count` before it is initialized, causing undefined behavior.

explanation_md: For a non-`inline` static member, the in-class `static int s_count;` is only a declaration â€” it does not allocate storage. A definition like `int Widget::s_count{ 0 };` outside the class body is required. Without it, the linker reports an undefined symbol. The fix is to add `int Widget::s_count{ 0 };` at namespace scope, or to change the declaration to `static inline int s_count{ 0 };`.
